cards.back: ~14th-17th century Europe, starting in Italy — art and science revival.
Ancient trade routes linking China to Europe via Central Asia.
Commonly seven continents and five oceans.
An 1884 conference set it as the 0° prime meridian.

cards.front: Why did the printing press matter?
cards.back: Gutenberg (~1440) made books cheap, spreading literacy and ideas fast.
Began in Britain in the late 1700s — machines and factories.
1945 — Europe in May, the Pacific in September.
Apollo 11 landed the first humans on the Moon.

cards.front: When did the Western Roman Empire fall?
cards.back: 476 CE; the Eastern (Byzantine) lasted ~1000 years more.

cards.front: What was the Great Wall for?
cards.back: To slow northern nomadic raids and control movement/trade.
Royal tombs built ~4,500 years ago in ancient Egypt.
Athens, ~5th century BCE — citizens voted directly on laws.
A 14th-century plague that killed roughly a third of Europe.
Columbus reached the Americas, linking the hemispheres (the Columbian Exchange).
1789 — it overthrew the monarchy and spread ideas of citizen rights.
A ~1947-1991 standoff between the US and USSR — rivalry without direct war.

cards.front: When did the Berlin Wall fall?
cards.back: 1989, signaling the end of the Cold War and Germany's reunification.
In China, by alchemists — later transforming warfare worldwide.
Sumer (Mesopotamia), as cuneiform ~5,000 years ago.
A 1215 English charter that limited the king's power under law.
1914-1918 — triggered in Europe and reshaping the map and modern warfare.

cards.front: Why call Egypt "the gift of the Nile"?
cards.back: The river's annual floods made the desert farmable, sustaining the civilization.
Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo — masters of art and invention.
